chapters.title: Technology has changed the way we date and mate
chapters.text:
In the form of effective contraceptives, technological advanceshave stunned the cost–benefit analysis of sex. Millions of women and men think daily about having noncommittal sex because we now have the power to do so with little to no consequences. In the past, people had a fear of unwanted pregnancies and STDs to consider before having sex. But not anymore.

According to a 2010 report by Pew Research, about 44% of young adults see marriage as an obsolete institution.

Let’s talk about birth control technology. Since women can control their fertility, most have concluded that the benefits of premarital sex outweigh the costs. It is now safer to have sex with less risk of pregnancy. Ages ago, a woman knew that if she had an illegitimate child, her chances of marrying a man with a good income became very slim. But even with advances in contraceptive technology, the number of unwanted pregnancies keeps increasing. But this doesn’t mean sex tech is not important; it simply means that it spurred social change leading to a rise in promiscuity.

Does promiscuity have any benefits? Sex makes people happy, so you would think people with more sexual partners would be happier. But that’s far from accurate. Research shows the more sexual partners you have, the less happy you will be. The economic approach to explaining human sexuality enables us to understand why we went through the liberalization of sexual values in the twentieth century and how the ever–widening gaps in incomes between the poor and the rich have led to increasing unintended pregnancies in impoverished communities. The remaining chapters of this summary are all about exploring these points.

chapters.title: College students have different sexual experiences from their age mates who don’t attend school
chapters.text:
Contrary to common belief, university students have less sex on average than their counterparts who aren’t studying. Let’s look at the fate of most students on campus. On Thursday nights, students are binge drinking at a bar off–campus. Most women will go to this bar just to have a good time with their friends, while others go there and use their drunken state to hook up with any willing guy. Only a few go there to enjoy male attention while seeking a relationship, but this proves impossible since most men won’t want to be with a woman who doesn’t give up the cookie within a few hours after they meet.

Another popular misconception is that men like sex more than women. The truth is that men prefer having several sexual partners; meanwhile, women prefer being assured that sex is not a one–off experience. In one study, researchers asked people how many sexual partners they would like to have over the next two years. Women on average preferred just one partner, while men wanted eight sexual partners on average.

The desire in men to be with several partners and their willingness to partake in anonymous sex fuel the sex trade around the world.

Of recent, university campuses have turned into cool places for young men looking for casual sex. The high number of women vs. men on campus makes it hard for women to get a suitable partner. The available men seem to have acquired greater sex–market power. The women know that not giving it up on the first night means that a guy will likely not date her because other women are available and willing to take that risk. Therefore, women lose their ability to negotiate when and how the couple should have their first sexual encounter. An abundance of women on campus means less traditional dating and more casual hook–ups.

Did you know? According to research by Economist Jeffrey DeSimone, binge drinking is a significant factor that contributes to risky sexual behavior on university campuses. For instance, binge drinkers are 25% more likely to be sexually active, 20% more likely to have unprotected sex, and 94% more likely to sleep with multiple partners.

chapters.title: The filters on online dating sites make it easier to find love, and many potential partners are discarded because of flimsy features
chapters.text:
When people face too many options on online dating sites, they often choose to simplify by using a process of elimination, skimming over large categories of possibilities to streamline their decision–making process. So, they reject possible partners without ever seeing them, not of who they are, but because they couldn’t escape the filtered search. Meanwhile, such people might have been the perfect fit for them.

Online searches encourage us to look for qualities that we can easily measure, like education, height, age, income, and race. The caliber of a relationship depends more on the qualities that can’t be easily measured or quantified in an online search.

From an economics viewpoint, limited searches turn thick markets into artificially thin markets. The matches on the narrow market are likely to be of low quality.

At the end of the market for spouses, men and women do not necessarily marry the type of person their initial preferences dictate: they marry the person who is both available and willing to be married to them. ~ Marina Adshade, PhD

To understand certain preferences in dating, we have to analyze data collected while the markets are in action. For instance, multiple researchers have shown that a racially segregated marriage market is driven mainlyby women's same–race preference; men often show less same–race preference in dating.

The dating market is just like any other market that consists of sellers and buyers, and it is only in equilibrium when sellers and buyers are willing to trade after the price has adjusted. There is no money exchange in this market. Prices are determined by opportunity costs that people accept when they eventually meet a person they choose to date.

Struggling to determine your value in the dating market might lead to missing out on the economic benefits of marriage.

The fastest way to get off the market and find a mate is to price one appropriately, i.e., accurately estimate your values on the market. In general, people are biased when assessing themselves. We all think we are sexier, funnier, smarter, and kinder than the average person. The next time you hop onto an online dating site, be honest with yourself. Rate your physical appearance compared to people on the site who are the same age and gender as you.

chapters.title: Your value on the market limits who you are with when your market closes
chapters.text:
Though you might not get what you want, the person with the same market value as you might be who you need. People marry for various reasons, but from an economic perspective, marriage is solely for insurance in bad times and the efficient production of household services or goods, such as sex, love, and children.

Marriage produces several goods and services, and most serve as a cost–effective and efficient way for couples to have children in a safe and nurturing environment. Married couples without children enjoy other benefits like a stable income, a clean house, and home–cooked meals. The most efficient marriage is one in which the couple excels in different activities so they can bring different talents into the relationship. For example, a person who loves cooking and taking care of the home will be better off marrying a person with a comparative advantage in earning a high income to run the home.

Single people often relocate to large cities because the search costs become lower in highly populated areas, and the quality of potential matches is higher. In large cities, singles will be able to come in contact with a lot more people than in less–populated rural areas where people might be skeptical about speaking to strangers.

If you can't find a suitable partner where you are, consider moving to a bigger city where you'll meet more people.

Abhijit Banerjee et al. researched marriage and education in India and recorded their findings in the American Economic Journal. According to the study, most men prefer marrying a woman who is less educated than they are. The educated woman will also be older by the time she starts looking for a husband, so she faces competition from younger and less–educated women. When educated women struggle to find potential mates, the market is out of equilibrium because supply is greater than demand. ­­

chapters.title: The meanings we give to marriage varies across religious and geographical divisions
chapters.text:
The meaning or definition of marriage is not engraved in stone; it varies from one place to another, from country to country, and over time. Communities decide the institution of marriage, but economic factors also affect its evolution. For instance, heterosexual monogamy polygyny has been the widely accepted definition of marriage. However, other forms of marriages, such as polyamory, polyandry, and serial monogamy, are recognized today.

Respect for individual rights and freedom is one of the reasons that some nations have been able to accumulate wealth and experience progress in new technologies. And the same respect for human rights has enabled advocates for same–sex monogamous marriages to win legal battles for legal recognition. When the citizens of a country disagree with a type of marriage, a change in the legal framework is all that is needed for institutional change to occur.

Over the last decade, public opinion of same–sex relationships has changed rapidly, and people are more open to changing their beliefs. Through the evolution of these beliefs, institutional change occurs. Open acceptance of this trend, called the cohort effect, is due to the accepting nature of the younger generations that make up most of the population.

If you want to get any ideology to the mainstream, just try to make sure a high enough number of energetic young people buy into it.

Societies in developed and first–world countries have rejected polygamy as a marriage institution because people prefer to have a few educated children over several less–educated children. Monogamy helps them achieve that goal, so society accepted the opinion that a monogamous marriage is better than a polygamous one.

chapters.title: The happiest marriages are those in which husbands and wives are similar to each other
chapters.text:
In the past 30 years, several economic factors have leveled the formal labor market for both men and women, enabling more women to have higher bargaining power in their marriages. The income gap between husbands and wives seems to be shrinking, but the income gap between poor and rich is widening.

Some economists argue that the closing economic gap between men and women could be responsible for increasing divorce rates.

Some people get married to exploit the gains from trade. A good example of such people are men that import wives from another country in an attempt to discard domestic economic forces that give women a say in their marriages. Negotiation is an essential part of decision–making between couples ⁠— from resource allocation to household chores.

Technology advances have given women more freedom to spend time on their careers and get better earnings in the job market. Several services previously done by women at home can now be purchased. Higher incomes mean women who are tired of losing negotiations in their marriage can choose to leave and still be financially stable. Any man that doesn’t want to share decision–making is at risk of divorce.

However, most men do not want to share decision–making with their wives. They want a good wife who sees them as the lord of the home who would not question his authority. They achieve this goal by looking for wives in marriage markets in developing countries where women are often disadvantaged in the labor market. International marriage brokers convince men to seek submissive foreign women. Conflict in expectations between the two parties in these relationships often leads to high rates of divorce and domestic abuse.

The great marriages are partnerships. It can't be a great marriage without being a partnership. ~ Helen Mirren

Research studies have shown that the happiest couples are those in which both husband and wife are foreign–born or native–born.

chapters.title: People are starting relationships at different stages of their lives, and they are living in a way that differs from the past
chapters.text:
The dynamics of relationship styles have changed over the years. For instance, people are more willing to live in cohabitation instead of getting married, making the late–in–life market for love and sex much thicker than before.

Most people choose to get married late since they want fewer children; getting married after 30 means that most women will likely have only one to two kids. As lifestyle choices and fertility treatments can enable women to have kids in their late 30s and early 40s, they no longer feel pressured to marry early. Changing societal norms and effective contraceptives have led to increased access to premarital sex.

People don’t have to get married to have sex anymore. This freedom means that singles can keep multiple sexual partners until they are ready to make a long–term commitment.

Advances in home production technology have made everyone self–sufficient ⁠— women can work for wages instead of staying at home, and men don’t need women for goods and services. These economic forces have destigmatized childbirth outside marriage. Women can live as single mothers and raise their children while remaining unmarried. Therefore, women who become mothers as teens marry later in life.

Another reason for late–in–life dating is that it is easier and cheaper to search for mates, thanks to the widespread use of social networking and online dating sites. In the past, older singles tended to spend more to find a mate since they were usually more socially isolated than younger singles.

Finally, people have a longer life span. In 1960, a woman was likely to live until 80 years old, while a man was likely to live until 75. Now, people have added to their life span by four to six years, and longer life means people are more willing to spend time searching for new love.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The world of sex and love is not just a sea of emotions and desires. Our decisions and their consequences are more sensible when we evaluate them from an economic perspective. Luckily, research studies and financial analysis have unlocked the mystery behind our preferences, thoughts, and actions. We are left to choose howthisdata will impact our personal and romantic lives. Our decisions to have a sexual partner at a certain age, get married, or get divorced boil down to cost analysis. In other words, what will I lose if I follow the norm or choose to go another way?

It's all about the dollar. People choose to marry partners of equal or higher economic status than they are because they don't want to deal with unnecessary financial pressure. And those who decide to keep marriage at bay do so because they can have access to as much sex as they want, with as many partners as they want, sans the commitment that marriage demands. Choosing to stay single also means more time to focus on career, wealth creation, and every other thing that's important to them.

After reading this summary, it's up to you to choose the romantic life you want to live. Whatever your preference is, just keep the economics of dating and marriage in mind.

You know it's never fifty–fifty in a marriage. It's always seventy–thirty, or sixty–forty. Someone falls in love first. Someone puts someone else up on a pedestal. Someone works very hard to keep things rolling smoothly; someone else sails along for the ride. ~ Jodi Picoult

Try this

Determine your position in the market by giving yourself an honest score on a scale of one to ten regarding physical appearances for your age and gender. Look for a partner with the same qualities as you, even though that might sound counterintuitive. If you struggle to find love in your city, it is time to relocate to a bigger city where your options will increase.

Read it till the end?
